tasks.prompt: Warm-up: Write a Dockerfile for a minimal Node.js web application that demonstrates correct layer caching order. The app listens on port 3000 and starts with 'node server.js'. Use the 'node:18-alpine' base image. Explain in a comment why you copy 'package*.json' before copying the rest of the source.
tasks.starter:
# Create a file named Dockerfile in your project root.
# The project directory contains: server.js  package.json  package-lock.json

# Your Dockerfile here:
tasks.solution:
FROM node:18-alpine
WORKDIR /app
# Copy dependency manifests first so Docker can cache the install layer.
# If only server.js changes, this layer is reused and npm install is skipped.
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm install
COPY . .
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
tasks.explanation: Docker builds images layer by layer and caches each layer. When you re-build, layers whose inputs have not changed are served from cache. By copying 'package*.json' and running 'npm install' before copying the application source, the expensive install step is only re-executed when the dependency list changes. A change to 'server.js' alone only invalidates the final 'COPY . .' layer and beyond, so builds are much faster. Reversing the order — 'COPY . .' first — would invalidate the install cache on every source change, even when no dependency changed.
hints: The key is order: Docker invalidates all layers after the first changed layer. Think about which files change most often (source code) vs. least often (package.json).
EXPOSE does not actually publish the port; it documents intent. The real port mapping happens at 'docker run -p'.
CMD uses JSON array form (exec form) to avoid shell processing: CMD ["node", "server.js"] rather than CMD node server.js.

tasks.prompt: Core practice A — Create an ECR repository and authenticate Docker to it. Using the AWS CLI: (1) create a private ECR repository named 'my-app' in the 'us-east-1' region, (2) authenticate your local Docker daemon to that registry. Replace '<account-id>' with your actual 12-digit AWS account ID throughout.
tasks.starter:
# Step 1: create the ECR repository
aws ecr create-repository \
  --repository-name ________ \
  --region ________

# Step 2: authenticate Docker
aws ecr get-login-password --region ________ \
  | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin \
    ________.dkr.ecr.________.amazonaws.com
tasks.solution:
# Step 1: create the ECR repository
aws ecr create-repository \
  --repository-name my-app \
  --region us-east-1

# Step 2: authenticate Docker
aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 \
  | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin \
    <account-id>.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
tasks.explanation: 'aws ecr get-login-password' requests a short-lived authorization token from AWS and writes it to stdout. The pipe passes it directly to 'docker login' using '--password-stdin', so the secret never appears in your shell history. The registry URL format is always '<account-id>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com' — this identifies your account's ECR endpoint in that region. The login token expires after 12 hours, so production pipelines re-authenticate at the start of each build.
hints: If you get an 'UnauthorizedException', check that your AWS CLI is configured with credentials that have 'ecr:GetAuthorizationToken' and 'ecr:CreateRepository' permissions.
The 12-digit account ID is visible in the top-right corner of the AWS Console, or via: aws sts get-caller-identity --query Account --output text
You can verify the repository was created with: aws ecr describe-repositories --region us-east-1

tasks.prompt: Core practice B — Build, tag with the current Git commit SHA, and push the image to ECR. Assume your ECR repository URL is '<account-id>.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-app'. Write the four commands for: build → tag with commit SHA → push → verify the tag exists in ECR.
tasks.starter:
# Build
docker build -t my-app .

# Tag with the short commit SHA (fill in the git command)
docker tag my-app:latest \
  <account-id>.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-app:________

# Push
docker push \
  ________

# Verify the image tag exists in ECR
aws ecr describe-images \
  --repository-name my-app \
  --region us-east-1
tasks.solution:
# Build
docker build -t my-app .

# Tag with the short commit SHA
docker tag my-app:latest \
  <account-id>.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-app:$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)

# Push
docker push \
  <account-id>.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-app:$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)

# Verify the image tag exists in ECR
aws ecr describe-images \
  --repository-name my-app \
  --region us-east-1
tasks.explanation: 'git rev-parse --short HEAD' outputs the short (7-character) SHA of the current commit, e.g. 'a1b9f3c'. Using this as the image tag creates an immutable, traceable artifact: the tag tells you exactly which commit produced this image. The 'docker tag' command creates an alias — it does not copy the image; it simply adds another name pointing to the same layers. 'docker push' then uploads the layers to ECR under that full registry/repository:tag address. The 'describe-images' call confirms the tag appears in ECR and is ready to be referenced in a Kubernetes Deployment manifest.
hints: You can also capture the SHA in a variable for readability: IMAGE_TAG=$(git rev-parse --short HEAD) then use $IMAGE_TAG in the tag and push commands.
If 'docker push' returns 'no basic auth credentials', you need to re-run the 'aws ecr get-login-password' pipe from the previous task — the 12-hour token may have expired.
The full ECR image name format is: <account-id>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com/<repo-name>:<tag> — every part is required.

tasks.prompt: Applied challenge — Convert the single-stage Dockerfile from the warm-up into a multi-stage build that produces a smaller final image. Stage 1 (named 'builder') installs all dependencies including devDependencies. Stage 2 (final image) starts from 'node:18-alpine', copies only the production node_modules and the application source from stage 1, and sets the same CMD. Explain why the final image is smaller.
tasks.starter:
# Multi-stage Dockerfile
# Stage 1: build stage
FROM node:18-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
# ... install all deps including devDependencies

# Stage 2: production image
FROM ________
WORKDIR /app
# Copy only what the running app needs from the builder stage
# COPY --from=builder ...

EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
tasks.solution:
# Stage 1: install all dependencies (including dev)
FROM node:18-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm install
COPY . .

# Stage 2: production image — only runtime artifacts
FROM node:18-alpine
WORKDIR /app
# Copy only production node_modules and application code from builder
COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY --from=builder /app/server.js ./server.js
# If you have additional source files/directories, copy them too:
# COPY --from=builder /app/src ./src
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
tasks.explanation: In a single-stage build, every tool used during the build (compilers, test runners, devDependencies) ends up baked into the final image. Multi-stage builds fix this: only the artifacts that are explicitly COPY --from= transferred into the final stage are included. The final image contains no build toolchain, no devDependencies, and no intermediate build files — just the minimal runtime. In practice this typically cuts image size by 30–70%, reducing both pull time and the attack surface (fewer packages = fewer potential vulnerabilities). The '--from=builder' syntax names which stage to copy from.
hints: npm install with no flags installs both 'dependencies' and 'devDependencies'. To install only production deps in stage 2 you could alternatively run 'npm install --omit=dev' in the second stage instead of copying node_modules.
You can copy entire directories: COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist — useful for compiled TypeScript or React builds.
After building, compare image sizes: docker images my-app — compare the multi-stage result to the size of the original single-stage image.
